Facts.text: Army of Cyrus the Great of Persia takes Babylon
Battle of Hatfield Chase: King Edwin of Northumbrian defeated and killed by an alliance of Welsh kingdom Gwynedd led by Cadwallon ap Cadfan, and Mercia led by Penda
Honorius I ends his reign as Catholic Pope; Pope Severinus elected
John IV ends his reign as Catholic Pope
Maurice de Sully is elected bishop of Paris, will initiate plan to build Notre Dame
Jewish rabbi and philosopher Moses ben Maimon (aka Maimonides or the Rambam) reaches Jerusalem
Nichiren, a Japanese Buddhist monk, founder of Nichiren Buddhism, inscribes the Dai-Gohonzon

180 Jews burn to death as synagogue in which they are hiding set ablaze by mob of Christians in retribution for alleged crime of "blood libel", ritualistic bloodletting from a kidnapped child, in Munich, Duchy of Upper Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire
King Frederick III of Siciliy forbids decorations on synagogues
The Treaty of Salynas is signed between Grand Duke of Lithuania Vytautas the Great and the Teutonic Knights, who received Samogitia.
Battle at Ludford Bridge: Richard of York defeated
Flemish rebel leader Philip van Kleef surrenders
Holy RomanEmperor Maximilian Ileaves Italy
John Dudley, Earl of Warwick, forces the Duke of Somerset out of his office as Lord Protector.
Children's rhyme "Three Blind Mice" is published in London in a book edited by and possibly written by Thomas Ravenscroft
The Delft Explosion (gunpowder) devastates the city of Delft in the Netherlands, killing more than 100 people
English "Rump" Parliament dismisses John Lambert and other Parliamentary generals
William IV Prince of Orange installed as Stadtholder of Gelderland
Christian VI succeeds Frederik IV as king of Denmark
America's first asylum opens for 'Persons of Insane and Disordered Minds' in Virginia
British Brigade begins guarding Throgg Necks Road in Bronx
First celebration ofColumbus Dayin the USA is held in New York
Flemish uprising against French occupied Boerenkrijg
Friedrich von Schiller's play "Wallensteins Lager" premieres in Weimar
Second eruption of Galunggung in Java destroys summit of mountain
Charles Macintosh of Scotland begins selling raincoats (Macs)
18" of snow falls in Bridgewater, NY
John Morrissey wins boxing title, when Yankee Sullivan leaves ring after 36th round to slug Morrissey's fans
Ashmun Institute (later Lincoln University) opens near Oxford, Pennsylvania
British & French troops capture Beijing
Confederate ironclad Manassas attack Union's Richmond on Mississippi
Major General Earl Van Dorn assumes command of Confederate troops in Mississippi
Skirmish at Monocacy, Maryland
US President Grant condemns Ku Klux Klan
Montreal Foot Ball Club 1st game playing Quebec City to 0-0 tie
British troops occupy Kabul, Afghanistan
Henry M Stanley signs contract with Congo monarch
Hurricane & sea surge kills 250 at Indianola Texas
Astronomical Society of France is inaugurated
US Pledge of Allegiance first recited in public schools during Columbus Day
Establishment of the first town council in Mateur.
South Africa Boer Republic declares war on Britain
Lyric Theater opens at 213 W 42nd St NYC
The First Battle of Ypres begins
Theodore Rooseveltcriticizes US citizens who identify themselves with dual nationalities
Cloquet fire kills 453 and injures or displaces 52,000 people
Construction begins on Holland Tunnel connecting NJ & NYC
Man O'War's last race & win
Sokolnicheskaya Radio begins broadcasting from Moscow
Albert Michelsen runs world record marathon (2:19:01.8)
German-Russian trade agreement signed
Hermann Goerner of Germany raises 24 men weighing 4,123 lbs on a plank with soles of his feet
1st use of iron lung (Boston's Children Hospital)
1st International Conference on Calendar Reform
Alcatraz becomes a federal prison (unofficially)
Soviet government moves from Moscow to Volga as Nazis forces close in on Moscow
Successful Soviet counterattack through 37th Guard division
WWII: US Navy defeats Japanese in Battle of Cape Esperance between Savo Island and Guadalcanal in theSouth Pacific's Solomon Islands
US bombs Rabaul, New Britain
German army retreats from Athens
KBTV (now KUSA) TV channel 9 in Denver, CO (ABC) begins broadcasting
US & Greece sign peace treaty (US bases)
Marga Klompé becomes first Dutch women elected minister
First commercial flight between California & Antarctica
CVP wins municipal elections in Belgium
USSR performs nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya USSR
WJRT TV channel 12 in Flint, MI (ABC) begins broadcasting
At the national congress of APRA in Peru a group of leftist radicals are expelled from the party. They will later form APRA Rebelde.
Japanese politician Inejiro Asanuma is assassinated with a sword during a televised debate live on TV by 17-year old nationalist Otoya Yamaguchi
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Infamous Columbus Day Storm strikes the U.S. Pacific Northwest with record wind velocities; 46 dead and at least U.S. $230 million in damages
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
US/USSR sign joint space effort in telecommunications & meteorology
Archaeological digs begin at Masada, Israel
Final event at Polo Grounds, New York City; 14,235 attend 1st (and only) "Latino All-Star Game" - National League wins 5-2 over American League; festivities include a concert by Tito Puente
San Francisco's Oakland Bay Bridge converted to 1-way on each deck
American Don Schollander swims an Olympic record 53.4s to beat Briton Robert McGregor by 0.1s and win the 100m freestyle gold medal in Tokyo; first of Schollander's 4 gold medals at the Games
KCSM TV channel 60 in San Mateo-SF, CA (PBS) begins
Launch of Voskhod 1, first time 3 people in space (Komarov, Feokistov, Yegorov)
WSBK TV channel 38 in Boston, MA (IND/ABC/CBS/NBC) begin
WUCM TV channel 19 in University Center, MI (PBS) begins broadcasting
Enriqueta Basilio, Mexican track and field athlete becomes 1st woman to light Olympic flame at 19th Summer Olympics, in Mexico City, Mexico
Equatorial Guinea (Spanish Guinea) declares independence from Spain
IXX Summer Olympic Games open at Mexico City, Mexico; first Olympics in Latin America
1st time 5 people in space simultaneously (Soyez 6 & Soyez 7)
KDB-AM in Santa Barbara, California changes call letters to KAPN
KHOF (now KAGL) TV channel 30 in San Bernardino/Glenda, California (IND) begins
Soviet spacecraft Soyuz 7 is launched from Baikonur Cosmodrome in southern Kazakhstan
Rock Memorabilia Auction at Filmore East
46 sailors injured in race riot on American aircraft carrier USS Kitty Hawk
Mariner 9 takes pictures of Martian north pole
Israeli counter offensive in southern Syria
NHL Washington Capitals suffer 1st franchise shutout, losing 6-0 to Minnesota North Stars
American Jacqueline Hansen runs women's world record marathon 2:38:19 in the Nike OTC Marathon, Eugene, Oregon
Archbishop Oliver Plunkett, martyred in 1681, is canonized in Rome, becoming the first Irish-born saint in seven centuries
NASA launches space vehicle S-195
"Throwaway Game" Catfish Hunter loses WS 6-1 resting Yank pitchers
4th test of space shuttle Enterprise
Psychic Romark attempts to drive blindfolded, smashed into cop van
US Supreme Court heard arguments in "reverse discrimination" case of Allan Bakke, white student denied admission to University of California Med School
Representatives of Israel & Egypt open talks in Washington
The lowest recorded non-tornadic atmospheric pressure, 87.0 kPa (870 mbar or 25.69 inHg), occurred in Guam, in the Western Pacific during Typhoon Tip
Typhoon Tip becomes the most intense tropical cyclone ever recorded with peak sustained winds of 305km/h (190mph) and a wind diameter of 2,220km (1,380mi)
7 stabbed in a fight at Blood, Sweat & Tears concert in Los Angeles
38.6 cm (15.2") of rainfall, Angoon Alaska (state record)
Challenger moves to Vehicle Assembly Building at Kennedy Space Center (Florida) for maintenance of STS 61A mission
Intl Physicians for Prevention of Nuclear War receives Nobel Prize
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
California Angels within 1 pitch of pennant victory lose to Red Sox
Oscar Arias (Costa Rican Pres) wins Nobel Peace Prize
Israel & China sign trade deal, plan diplomatic relations
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Boxer Hector Comacho arrested for driving while getting oral sex
Mike Lerch of Princeton ties NCAA record with 370 receiving yards
Professional wrestler Rip is Oliver forced to retire after being injured by Crush
Statler Brothers Show premieres on TNN
5.8 earthquake at Cairo (at least 510 die)
Arecibo radio telescope begins Microwave Observing Project search for occupied planets
Expo '92 closes in Seville Spain
Howard Stern Radio Show premieres in Albany, New York on  WQBK 103.5 & 103.9 FM
Wash Redskin Art Monk sets NFL record with 820th reception
Contact with NASA Venus orbitter Magellan broken
Iranian Fokker F28 explodes between Isfahan and Tehran, killing 66
Sidi Daoud massacre in Algeria; 43 killed at a fake roadblock
The Day of Six Billion is proclaimed as the 6 billionth living human in the world is born
American destroyer USS Cole is badly damaged in Aden, Yemen, by two suicide bombers, killing 17 crew members and wounding at least 39
Terrorists explode two bombs in Bali's nightclub district killing 202 and injuring 209 mostly foreign tourists
FIFA Women's World Cup Final, Home Depot Center, Carson, CA: Nia Künzer scores winner in sudden death extra time as Germany beats Sweden, 2-1
The second Chinese human spaceflight Shenzhou 6 launched carrying Fèi Jùnlóng and Niè Hǎishèng for five days in orbit.
United States Postal Service designates facility in Green River, Wyoming, as the "Curt Gowdy Post Office Building," honoring the noted broadcaster
The European Union wins the 2012 Nobel Peace Prize, sparking a series of critical commentary
The United Nations Security Council unanimously approves of an African-led force to oust Islamist militants in northern Mali
15 people are killed by a series of explosions in a fireworks factory in Vietnam
50 people are killed after a truck veers off of a cliff in La Convencion Province, Peru
Timothy Bradley defeats Juan Manuel Márquez to win the WBA Welterweight boxing title
Auston Matthews becomes 1st player to score 4 goals in his NHL debut for the Toronto Maple Leafs at Ottawa
James Charles (17) becomes the 1st male face of make-up label CoverGirl
Plague outbreak in Madagascar kills 57, with 684 cases so far reported by WHO
US air strike in central Somalia kills about 60 al-Shabab militants
California is the 1st US state to pass law banning sale and manufacture of new fur products
Typhoon Hagibis makes landfall on the Izu Peninsula near Tokyo, Japan, bringing record rainfall and killing at least 56 people
China announces it will test entire city of Qingdao, 9 million people, for COVID-19 within five days after a dozen cases discovered
Nobel Prize for Economics awarded to Paul Milgrom and Robert Wilson for their contributions to auction theory
Lebanon reports its first death from Cholera since 1993, spreading from neighbouring Syria
UN General Assembly passes a resolution condemning Russia's attempted annexation of Ukrainian territory
Israel says it will not stop the bombardment of Gaza until hostages released, amid growing Gaza humanitarian crisis with 338,000 displaced and population without electricity or water
Roman scrolls burnt in Mt Vesuvius eruption at Herculaneum read for the first time, after computer-science student develops machine-learning algorithm - first word deciphered is purple
Spanish researchers, after studying genetic material, revealChristopher Columbuswas probably Spanish and Jewish and born in Valencia, not an Italian from Genoa as he claimed
King Johnof England loses his crown jewels in The Wash as the flood tide swamps his wagons, probably near Fosdyke, perhaps near Sutton Bridge
Christopher Columbus's expedition makes landfall on a Caribbean island he names San Salvador (likely Watling Island, Bahamas). The explorer believes he has reached East Asia (OS 21 Oct)
QueenIsabella Iof Spain signs her last will and testament, instructing her descendants to treat indigenous Americans well and fairly
Rudolf II succeedsMaximilian IIas Holy Roman Emperor
Charles VIis crowned Holy Roman Emperor in Frankfurt
English evangelistGeorge Whitefieldpreaches to 30,000 people in Boston in the US during the 'Great Awakening'
French Revolution: KingLouis XVIwrites secretly to the King of Spain about complaining of harsh treatment; the Count of Artois writes to the Austrian king requesting military intervention in France
Ex-King of NaplesJoachim Muratis sentenced to death for treason
The Empire of Brazil is inaugurated and Prince Pedro of Portugal is proclaimed Emperor DomPedro Ion his 24th birthday
Self-proclaimed Emperor of the USA,Emperor Nortonissues edict abolishing the US Congress
Confederate GeneralJ.E.B. Stuartcompletes his "secondride around McClellan" during the Raid on Chambersburg
Brazilian troops under the command of Gen. João Propício Mena Barreto invade Uruguay in a prelude to theWar of the Triple Alliance
Theodore Rooseveltrenames the "Executive Mansion" as "The White House"
Despite international protest,Edith Cavellan English nurse in Belgium, is executed by the Germans for aiding the escape of Allied prisoners
Franz Moissl conducts 1st performance ofAnton Bruckner's "Symphony No. 0" in Klosterneuburg, Austria
Gangster George Francis Barnes, akaMachine Gun Kelly, is sentenced to life imprisonment
John Dillingerescapes from jail in Allen County, Ohio
"Columbus Day Riot": 30,000 fans descend on NYC's Times Square for singerFrank Sinatra's performance at Paramount Theatre (capacity 3,500)
Casey Stengeltakes over as Yankee manager
The George Burns andGracie AllenShow debuts on CBS television, airs until 1958
JFKandRichard Nixon's third presidential debate
Soviet leaderNikita Khrushchevbangs his shoe on his desk at UN General Assembly session
Paramount Pictures and Motown Productions release film "Lady Sings the Blues", very loosely based on singerBillie Holiday's autobiography, starringDiana RossandBilly Dee Williams
US PresidentRichard NixonnominatesGerald Fordto replaceSpiro Agnewas Vice President
Sid Viciouscharged with murder of girlfriend Nancy Spungen
Boston Celtics guard Chris Ford scores first 3-point basket in NBA history in 1st quarter of 114-106 win v Houston at Boston Garden; game also marks debut of Boston rookieLarry Bird
Future Basketball Hall of Fame forwardMagic Johnsonmakes his debut for Los Angeles Lakers at the San Diego Clippers; Lakers win, 103-102
IRA bombs the Grand Hotel, Brighton, where British PMMargaret Thatcheris staying, 5 die
Elizabeth IIof the United Kingdom andPrince Philip,Duke of Edinburgh, visit the People's Republic of China
George Harrisonreleases single "Got My Mind Set On You", it becomes his third solo #1 hit, and the last chart-topper by any of the former Beatles
George H. W. Bush andMichael Dukakismeet in their second presidential debate
PopeJohn Paul IIis the target of anti-papal heckling by Protestant leaderIan Paisleyat a European Parliament meeting in Strasbourgh, France

Dallas running back Herschel Walker is traded from Cowboys to Minnesota Vikings for 5 players and 6 future draft picks including future starsEmmitt Smith, Russell Maryland, Kevin Smith and Darren Woodson
Jukebox musical "Buddy: TheBuddy HollyStory" with Paul Hipp, opens at the Victoria Palace Theatre in London's West End
Doug Flutieof BC Lions sets CFL passing record of 582 yards despite losing to Edmonton Eskimos, 45-38 in overtime
DreamWorks SKG film studio is founded by director and producerSteven Spielberg, former Disney executive Jeffrey Katzenberg, and music executiveDavid Geffen
Pat LaFontaineformally retires from the NHL due to lingering concussion problems
Pervez Musharraftakes power in Pakistan fromNawaz Sharifthrough a bloodless coup
German Ferrari driverMichael Schumacherfinishes 8th at the season-ending Japanese F1 Grand Prix at Suzuka; clinches 4th consecutive World Drivers Championship by 2 points fromKimi Räikkönen
Former US Vice PresidentAl Goreis awarded the Nobel Peace Prize (jointly with the IPCC)
Anna KournikovaandAndy RoddickdefeatMartina Navratilovaand Jesse Levine in a match for charity, raising over $400,000 for the Elton John AIDS Foundation and Atlanta AIDS Partnership Fund
Evo Moralesis re-elected President of Bolivia
Long-lost bust of Napoleon byAuguste Rodinconfirmed found in Madison borough hall, New Jersey, worth at least $4m
Centre-backSergio Ramosbecomes Europe's most capped male outfield soccer player (168 caps) as Spain draws 1-1 with Norway in Euro 2020 qualification in Oslo
Eliud Kipchogeof Kenya becomes first to run a marathon in under 2 hours (1:59:40) in Vienna, Austria; not a race by strict definition using pacesetters and pace laser markings on a doctored course
UK PMBoris Johnsonannounces a new three-tier system for COVID-19 restrictions as cases surge

American conspiracy theorist, radio host, and provocateurAlex Jonesordered by a Connecticut defamation trial jury to pay $965 million to families of those killed in 2012 shooting at Sandy Hook Elementary School for falsely and repeatedly claiming on his broadcasts that none of the 20 children and six adults killed had actually died and that their relatives were crisis actors
Concert film “Taylor Swift: The Eras Tour” premieres at the Grove Shopping Complex in Los Angeles
Papal envoy Cardinal Thomas Cajetan interrogatesMartin Lutherin Augsburg in a failed attempt to persuade Luther to revoke his theses
First modern submarine is commissioned by the US Navy as the USS Holland, named for its designerJohn Philip Holland
Ford Motor Company underHenry Fordmanufactures its 1 millionth automobile at the River Rouge plant in Detroit
Christ the Redeemer statue opens standing 30 meters high (98 ft) on top of Mount Corcovado overlooking Rio de Janeiro, built by engineer Heitor da Silva Costa
Yōmei, 31st Emperor of Japan (585-7), born in Japan (d. 587)
Go-Ichijō, 68th Emperor of Japan (1016-36), born in Heian Kyō, Japan (d. 1036)
Dmitry Donskoy, Grand Prince of Moscow (1359-89), born in Moscow (d. 1389)
Bernardo Pisano, Italian composer, born in Florence, Republic of Florence (d. 1548)
Jacques Sirmond, French scholar and Jesuit, born in Riom, France (d. 1651)
Maximilian III, Archduke of Austria (1612-18), born in Wiener Neustadt, Austria (d. 1618)
Thomas Dudley, British colonial governor of Massachusetts, born in Northamptonshire, England (d. 1653)
William Chillingworth, English scholar, theologian (The Religion of Protestants), and poet, born in Oxford, England (d. 1644)
Silvius Leopold Weiss, German composer and lutenist, born in Grodków, Poland (d. 1750)
Jonathan Trumbull, American politician and statesman during the American Revolutionary wars, Governor of Connecticut (1769-84), born in Lebanon, Connecticut (d. 1785)
William Shippen, American physician and delegate to the Continental Congress, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1801)
Etienne Louis Geoffroy, French pharmacist and entomologist, born in Paris (d. 1810)
Johann Peter Melchior, German sculptor, born in Lindorf, Germany (d. 1825)

John Ross, English composer and organist, born in Newcastle upon Tyne, England (d. 1837)
Lyman Beecher, US Presbyterian clergyman, temperance movement leader, born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 1863)
Christian Gmelin, German chemist, born in Tübingen, Germany (d. 1860)
Friedrich Frey-Herosé, Swiss politician, born in Lindau, Germany (d. 1873)
John Liptrot Hatton, English composer and pianist, born in Liverpool, England (d. 1886)
Lyman Trumbull, U.S. Senator from Illinois (1855-73), born in Colchester, Connecticut (d. 1896)
William J. Hardee, U.S. Army officer (Confederate Army), born in Camden County, Georgia (d. 1873)
Joseph Haydn Potter, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Concord, New Hampshire (d. 1892)

George W. Cable, American writer (Northampton Years), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1925)
Amintore Galli, Italian composer, artistic director, musical journalist, and music publisher, born in Perticara, Papal States (d. 1919)

Arthur Nikisch, Hungarian violinist, conductor (Berlin Philharmonic, 1895-1922; Leipzig Gewandhaus Orchestra, 1895-1922; Boston Symphony, 1893-95), and educator (Leipzig Conservatory, 1902-07), born in Mosonszentmiklós, Hungary (d. 1922)
August Sauer, Austrian literature historian (Euphorion), born in Wiener Neustadt, Austria (d. 1926)
Elmer Ambrose Sperry, American entrepreneur and inventor (gyrocompass), born in Cincinnatus, New York (d. 1930)
Arthur Harden, English biochemist (Nobel Prize 1929), born in Manchester, England (d. 1940)
Ralph Vaughan Williams, English composer (Hugh the Drover), born in Down Ampney, England (d. 1958)
Jimmy Burke, American baseball third baseman Pittsburgh Pirates; coach World Series 1932 NY Yankees; and manager St. Louis Cardinals 1905, Browns 1918-20, born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1942)
Aleister Crowley, British occultist, founded the religion of Thelema, born in Royal Leamington Spa, Warwickshire England (d. 1947)
Karl Buresch, Austrian lawyer and politician, born in Groß-Enzersdorf, Austria (d. 1936)
(James) Healey Willan, British-Canadian organist, composer, and educator, born in Balham, London, England (d. 1968)
Louis Hémon, French novelist (Maria Chapdelaine), born in Brest, France (d. 1913)
Carlos Lopez Buchardo, Argentine composer, born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1948)
William Nigh, American director (Ape, Doomed to Die, Mr Wong), born in Berlin, Wisconsin (d. 1955)
Hermann Wolfgang Sartorius Waltershausen, German musicologist and composer, born in Göttingen, Germany (d. 1954)
Pete Hill, American Baseball HOF outfielder (NgL lifetime batting average: .326) and manager (Detroit Stars, Baltimore Black Sox), born in Culpeper, Virginia (d. 1951)
Godfrey Tearle, English-American actor (39 Steps, Mandy, At Dawn We Die), born in New York City (d. 1953)
Karl Dane, Danish-American comedian and actor (Son of the Sheik), born in Copenhagen (d. 1934)
Paula von Preradović, Croatian-Austrian poet and writer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1951)
Luis de Freitas Branco, Portuguese composer, born in Lisbon, Portugal (d. 1955)

Páll Ísólfsson, Icelandic organist and composer, born in Stokkseyri, Iceland (d. 1974)

Eddie Myers, British WWII soldier and engineer, born in Kensington, London (d. 1997)
Willy Hess, Swiss musicologist, composer and famous Beethoven scholar, born in Winterthur, Switzerland (d. 1997)

Alice Childress, American playwright, actress and writer, born in Charleston, South Carolina (d. 1994)
Hans Bentz van den Berg, Dutch actor (Last Train), born in Blaricum, Netherlands (d. 1976)
Emil Cossetto, Croatian composer, born in Trieste, Austria-Hungary (d. 2006)
André Casanova, French composer, born in Paris, France (d. 2009)
Doris Miller, American US Navy cook and Pearl Harbor hero (Navy Cross), born in Waco, Texas (d. 1943)[1]
Gilles Beaudoin, Canadian politician (Mayor of Trois-Rivières), born in Trois-Rivières, Canada (d. 2007)
(James) "Lucky" Carmichael, American blues singer ("Hey Girl"), born in Harrodsburg, Kentucky (d. 1982)

Les Horvath, American College Football Hall of Fame quarterback and halfback (Heisman Trophy 1944; first Ohio State player to win the award; National C'ship 1942), born in South Bend, Indiana (d. 1995)
Goody Petronelli, American boxing trainer and manager, born in Brockton, Massachusetts (d. 2012)

Guitar Gabriel [Robert Lewis Jones], American blues singer and guitarist, born in Decatur, Georgia (d. 1996)
William Steinkraus, American equestrian (Olympic gold 1968, 2 silver 1960, 70, bronze 1952), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 2017)
César Pelli, Argentine American architect (Petronas Towers), born in San Miguel de Tucumán, Argentina (d. 2019)
Antonia Rey [Francesch], Cuban-born American actress (True Colors, Wise Guys), born in Havana, Cuba (d. 2019)
Al Held, American painter and sculptor (hard edge-style), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2005)
Jerzy Semkow, Polish-French conductor and educator, born in Radomsko (d. 2014)
Thomas Burnett Swann, American sci-fi author (Day of Minotaur), born in Tampa, Florida (d. 1976)
Brian Cobby, British actor (Evita, The Nudist Story) and the first male voice of the British speaking clock, born in Brighton, East Sussex, England (d. 2012)
Magnús Magnússon, Icelandic television presenter, born in Reykjavik, Iceland (d. 2007)
Nappy Brown [Napoleon Brown Culp], American R&B singer and songwriter ("Don't Be Angry", "Night Time Is the Right Time"), born in Charlotte, North Carolina (d. 2008)
Robert Coles, American child psychiatrist, Pulitizer Prize winning author (Children of Crisis), and educator, born in Boston, Massachusetts
Jack Gottlieb, American composer, writer and editor, born in New Rochelle, New York (d. 2011)
Mohamed Soukhane, Algerian soccer defender (6 caps; Le Havre AC), born in Algiers, Algeria (d. 2021)
Raoul Pleskow, Austrian-American composer, and educator (C.W. Post College, 1959-94), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2022)
Dick Gregory, American comedian, civil rights activist and dietician (Bahamian Diet), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 2017)
Dusan Makavejev, Serbian film director (Man is Not a Bird), born in Belgrade, Yugoslavia
Jake Edwin Garn, American politician (US Senate-R-Utah, 1974-93) and NASA astronaut (STS-51-D), born in Richfield, Utah
Joel Mandelbaum, American composer, and educator (Queens College at City University of New York, 1961-99), born in New York City
Ned Jarrett, American auto racer (NASCAR Cup Series 1961, 65; Motorsports HOF of America) and broadcaster (CBS, ESPN, TNN, FSN), born in Conover, North Carolina
Vitold Kreyer, Russian athlete (Olympic bronze, triple jump 1956, 60) and coach (Soviet athletics team 1967–80; head Russian team 2000 Olympics), born in Krasnodar, Russia (d. 2020)
Guido Molinari, Canadian painter, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2004)
Titus Buberník, Czech soccer midfielder (23 caps Czechoslovakia; ČH Bratislava, LASK Linz), born in Pusté Úľany, Czech Republic (d. 2022)
Albert Shiryaev, Russian mathematician, known for his work in probability theory, statistics and financial mathematics, born in Shchyolkovo, Russia
James "Sugar Boy" Crawford, American R&B singer, pianist, and songwriter ("Jock-A-Mo" aka "Iko Iko"), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 2012)
Richard Meier, American architect and abstract artist, born in Newark, New Jersey
Paul Humphrey, American jazz, R&B, and rock session and touring drummer, born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2014)
Sam Moore, American R&B and soul vocalist (Sam & Dave - "Soul Man", "I Thank You"), born in Miami, Florida (d. 2025)[1]
William Raspberry, American columnist (Pulitzer 1994), born in Okolona, Mississippi (d. 2012)
Tony Kubek, American baseball shortstop (MLB All-Star (1958, 59², 61, 61²; World Series 1958, 61, 62; NY Yankees) and sportscaster (NBC-TV, Madison Square Garden), born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin
Gawn Grainger, British theatre actor and playwright (Four to One), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 2025)[1]
Per Kristoffersen, Norwegian soccer forward (25 caps; Fredrikstad FC 194 games, 147 goals), born in Fredrikstad, Norway (d. 2023)
